content.text: Extended Thinking

is Claude's enhanced reasoning capability that lets the model work through complex problems step-by-step before delivering its final answer, while providing transparency into its internal thought process.
What It Is

attrs.alt: Diagram for What Is Extended Thinking

content.text: Extended Thinking gives Claude a "scratchpad" to reason through problems deliberately. When enabled, Claude outputs its internal reasoning in
thinking
content blocks, incorporates insights from that reasoning, then crafts a final response in
text
content blocks.
Why It Matters

Without extended thinking, Claude responds quickly with near-instant answers. With it, Claude self-reflects before answering, which significantly improves performance on math, physics, instruction-following, coding, and other tasks requiring deep reasoning.
Key Benefits
Benefit
Description
Enhanced reasoning
Handles multi-step reasoning, logical analysis, and complex problem solving
Transparency
You see Claude's step-by-step thought process via
thinking
blocks
Better accuracy
Self-reflection improves performance on math proofs, algorithm design, debugging
Authenticity verification
Thinking content includes digital signatures for verifying it was generated by Claude
How It Works
Enable it
: Set
thinking: {type: "enabled", budget_tokens: N}
in the API
Token budget
budget_tokens
determines how many tokens Claude can use for internal reasoning (e.g., 2,000 for simple problems, 10,000 for complex ones)
Response format
: API returns
thinking
blocks first, then
text
blocks
When to Use It
Use extended thinking for:

- Mathematical proofs and logical reasoning
- Algorithm design
- System architecture design requiring weighing multiple options
- Debugging complex issues with multi-level dependencies
- Strategic planning with multi-step reasoning
Don't use it for:

- Simple queries
- Creative writing
Trade-offs
Trade-off
Impact
Cost
Thinking tokens billed at output token price; total cost = input + thinking + output
Latency
Takes longer than instant responses due to deliberation
Not always needed
For tasks without deep reasoning, it adds cost without benefit
Supported Models
Extended thinking works on Claude Sonnet 4.5, Sonnet 4, Sonnet 3.7, Haiku 4.5, Opus 4.1, and Opus 4. On newer models like Claude Opus 4.6/4.7/4.8 and Claude Mythos,
adaptive thinking
is recommended instead, where the model decides dynamically how much to think.
Best Practices
Start with smaller
budget_tokens
(e.g., 2,000) for testing
Adjust budget based on task complexity
Monitor actual thinking content usage
Set
budget_tokens
less than
max_tokens

Overview

What Is Extended Thinking is a fundamental concept for anyone working with Claude and the Anthropic ecosystem. Understanding it deeply allows you to build more effective applications, write better prompts, and get more value from Claude's capabilities. This section provides a thorough exploration of the topic, covering both the theory and practical application aspects that you'll encounter in real-world usage.

Whether you're a beginner just starting out or an experienced developer looking to deepen your knowledge, this guide walks through everything you need to know about What Is Extended Thinking. We'll cover the core concepts, walk through concrete examples, address common questions, and show you how to apply what you've learned in practical scenarios.
Why This Matters

Understanding What Is Extended Thinking is important for several reasons. First, it forms the foundation for many other concepts in the Claude ecosystem. When you grasp this topic well, you'll find that more advanced subjects become significantly easier to understand and work with.

Second, practical knowledge of What Is Extended Thinking directly improves the quality and efficiency of your work. Whether you're building AI-powered applications, crafting prompts for specific use cases, or integrating Claude into existing systems, this knowledge pays dividends in every interaction.
Saves time by avoiding common mistakes and misunderstandings
Enables more effective use of Claude's capabilities for your specific needs
Provides the foundation for understanding more advanced topics
Helps you make better architectural and design decisions
Reduces troubleshooting time when issues arise in production
Improves communication with teammates about AI integration strategies
Core Concepts

To fully understand What Is Extended Thinking, it helps to break it down into its core components. Each component builds on the previous ones, creating a complete picture of how everything fits together.
Fundamental Principles

At its core, What Is Extended Thinking is about how Claude processes and responds to different types of inputs and contexts. The fundamental principles that govern this behavior are consistent across all use cases, even though the specific implementation details may vary.
Consistency: Claude applies the same underlying principles regardless of context or phrasing
Context sensitivity: The same input can produce different optimal outputs depending on the surrounding context
Iterative refinement: Most effective usage involves refining your approach based on results
Transparency: Claude's behavior follows predictable patterns that you can learn and leverage
Key Components

The key components of What Is Extended Thinking work together as an integrated system. Understanding each component individually is important, but understanding how they interact is what enables truly effective usage.
Input processing: How Claude receives and interprets your requests
Context management: How previous interactions influence current responses
Output generation: How Claude formulates and formats its responses
Quality control: Built-in mechanisms that ensure response quality and safety
Getting Started: Step-by-Step Guide
Here's a practical, step-by-step guide to getting started with What Is Extended Thinking. Follow these steps in order to build a solid foundation.
Set up your environment: Ensure you have access to Claude through the appropriate interface for your use case (Claude.ai, API, or Claude Code).
Understand the basics: Read through this documentation thoroughly before attempting your first implementation.
Start simple: Begin with the simplest possible example to verify your understanding is correct.
Iterate and experiment: Modify your approach based on results, keeping notes on what works and what doesn't.
Review best practices: Consult the best practices section below to align your approach with proven patterns.
Test edge cases: Once your basic implementation works, test it with edge cases and unusual inputs.
Scale gradually: Only add complexity when your simpler version is working reliably.
Practical Examples
The best way to understand What Is Extended Thinking is through concrete examples. These examples range from simple to complex, illustrating different aspects of the topic.
Basic Example
The following example demonstrates the most fundamental use case for What Is Extended Thinking. This is a great starting point for understanding the core behavior.

# Example: Getting started with What Is Extended Thinking
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Basic usage example
message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Explain What Is Extended Thinking in simple terms with a practical example."
        }
    ]
)

print(message.content[0].text)
Advanced Example
This more advanced example shows how to use What Is Extended Thinking in a real-world scenario with proper error handling, configuration, and best practices applied.

# Advanced example: Production-ready What Is Extended Thinking implementation
import anthropic
from typing import Optional
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class ClaudeWhatisextendedthinkiHandler:
    """Handles What Is Extended Thinking with production best practices."""

    def __init__(self, api_key: Optional[str] = None):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=api_key)
        self.model = "claude-opus-4-5"

    def process(self, input_text: str, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> str:
        """Process input with error handling and logging."""
        try:
            logger.info(f"Processing request of length {len(input_text)}")
            response = self.client.messages.create(
                model=self.model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]
            )
            result = response.content[0].text
            logger.info(f"Successfully generated response of length {len(result)}")
            return result
        except anthropic.RateLimitError:
            logger.warning("Rate limit reached, consider reducing request frequency")
            raise
        except anthropic.APIError as e:
            logger.error(f"API error: {e}")
            raise

# Usage
handler = ClaudeWhatisextendedthinkiHandler()
result = handler.process("Your query here")
print(result)
Comparison: Approaches and Trade-offs
Different approaches to What Is Extended Thinking have different trade-offs. This comparison helps you choose the right approach for your specific use case.

Less control, harder to scale

High-volume workflows
Efficient, scalable

Hybrid
Complex real-world systems
Flexible, best of both worlds
Requires careful design
Frequently Asked Questions
These are the most common questions about What Is Extended Thinking, based on developer feedback and support requests.
Q: What is the most important thing to understand about What Is Extended Thinking?

A: The most important thing to understand is that What Is Extended Thinking is a system that rewards careful, intentional usage. The more precisely you communicate your intent, the better results you'll get. Start with clear, specific inputs and refine based on the outputs you receive.
Q: How does this interact with other Claude features?

A: What Is Extended Thinking works together with most other Claude features. It's particularly important to understand how it interacts with system prompts, conversation history, and any tools or integrations you're using. In most cases, these interactions are synergistic rather than conflicting.
Q: What are the performance implications?

A: Performance depends on several factors: the complexity of your inputs, the length of your context window, and the Claude model you're using. Generally, simpler and more focused inputs produce faster and more accurate results. For high-throughput use cases, consider batching and caching strategies.
Q: How do I debug issues?

A: When debugging What Is Extended Thinking issues, start by isolating the problem. Simplify your input to the minimum that reproduces the issue, then gradually add complexity back. Check the API documentation for current behavior specifications and use logging to capture the full request-response cycle.
Best Practices
These best practices reflect lessons learned from production deployments and community experience with What Is Extended Thinking.
Be specific and explicit about what you want — vague inputs produce vague outputs
Test with representative examples from your actual use case before scaling
Implement proper error handling for all edge cases and API errors
Use the appropriate Claude model for your use case and budget constraints
Monitor usage and costs from day one to avoid unexpected bills
Keep your prompts and configurations in version control
Document your implementation decisions for future team members
Build in feedback loops to continuously improve your implementation
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Assuming one approach works for all use cases — always test with your specific requirements
Not handling errors — API calls can fail for many reasons; always handle failures gracefully
Over-complicating prompts — simpler is usually better; add complexity only when needed
Ignoring token limits — always account for input and output token constraints in your design
Not testing edge cases — unusual inputs often reveal important behavior patterns
Skipping monitoring — without metrics, you can't improve what you can't measure
Summary

This guide covered What Is Extended Thinking comprehensively: from core concepts and fundamental principles to practical examples, comparison of approaches, and best practices. The key insight is that effective usage comes from understanding the system deeply and applying that understanding consistently. Start simple, test thoroughly, and iterate based on results.

attrs.alt: Extended thinking flow: User prompt → Exploration → Self-Correction → Conclusion → Final Answer
attrs.title: Extended thinking flow: User prompt → Exploration → Self-Correction → Conclusion → Final Answer

content.text: The Think Before Answering Paradigm

Extended thinking gives Claude dedicated computation time to reason through a problem before committing to an answer. This mirrors how humans approach hard problems: we do not immediately answer complex questions but instead think, sketch ideas, reconsider, and only then respond. Claude's thinking process is made explicit and measurable through the budget_tokens parameter, which caps the number of tokens Claude can spend reasoning before generating its final response. Unlike Chain-of-Thought prompting — where you ask Claude to 'think step by step' and the reasoning appears inline in the response — extended thinking uses an architecturally separate reasoning phase. The thinking content is visible to you through the API but does not form part of the final response text, enabling clean separation between the reasoning process and the polished answer.

import anthropic
import time

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

problem = (
    "A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 die. "
    "How many sheep does the farmer have left? "
    "Show all your reasoning."
)

# Without thinking
t0 = time.time()
resp_no_think = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": problem}]
)
latency_no_think = time.time() - t0

# With thinking
t0 = time.time()
resp_think = client.beta.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
    max_tokens=1024,
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 5000},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": problem}],
    betas=["interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14"]
)
latency_think = time.time() - t0

# Compare
print("=== Without thinking ===")
print(resp_no_think.content[0].text)
print(f"Latency: {latency_no_think:.1f}s")

print("\n=== With thinking ===")
for block in resp_think.content:
    if block.type == "text":
        print(block.text)
print(f"Latency: {latency_think:.1f}s  (higher because reasoning runs first)")
How Thinking Tokens Work

Thinking tokens are consumed during Claude's internal reasoning phase and streamed as thinking content blocks with type 'thinking' in the API response, separate from the final text blocks. The budget_tokens parameter sets the maximum thinking tokens Claude may use; Claude will use fewer tokens if it reaches a confident conclusion early, so the budget is an upper bound rather than a fixed allocation. Thinking tokens are billed at the same rate as output tokens — which are typically priced higher than input tokens — so thinking budget directly affects cost. The minimum budget is 1,000 tokens and the maximum is 100,000 tokens. You can inspect actual thinking token consumption via the usage.thinking_tokens field in the response, which lets you calibrate budgets over time for different task categories.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

response = client.beta.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
    max_tokens=4096,
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 10000},
    messages=[{
        "role": "user",
        "content": "Prove that the square root of 2 is irrational."
    }],
    betas=["interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14"]
)

# Separate blocks by type
thinking_blocks = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "thinking"]
text_blocks = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "text"]

print(f"Response has {len(response.content)} content block(s):")
print(f"  Thinking blocks: {len(thinking_blocks)}")
print(f"  Text blocks:     {len(text_blocks)}")

# Inspect thinking content
for i, tb in enumerate(thinking_blocks):
    print(f"\nThinking block {i} ({len(tb.thinking)} chars):")
    preview = tb.thinking[:300] + "..." if len(tb.thinking) > 300 else tb.thinking
    print(preview)

# Inspect final answer
print("\n=== Final Answer ===")
for tb in text_blocks:
    print(tb.text)

# Inspect token usage
usage = response.usage
thinking_tokens = getattr(usage, "thinking_tokens", 0)
print(f"\nToken usage:")
print(f"  Input tokens:   {usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"  Thinking tokens: {thinking_tokens}  (of {10000} budget)")
print(f"  Output tokens:  {usage.output_tokens}")
print(f"  Budget consumed: {thinking_tokens / 10000 * 100:.1f}%")
What Happens Inside a Thinking Block

The thinking block content is Claude's raw stream-of-consciousness reasoning. Unlike the final answer, the thinking block is not polished for human reading — it may contain dead ends, false starts, self-corrections, and exploratory tangents that were ultimately abandoned. Inside a thinking block you might see: problem decomposition ('Let me break this into parts...'), hypothesis testing ('If X is true, then Y would follow...'), error checking ('Wait, I made an error above — let me recalculate...'), and conclusion synthesis ('So the answer must be...'). This raw reasoning is precisely what enables Claude to solve problems that would defeat a straightforward one-pass answer. The self-correction mechanism in particular is valuable: Claude can recognize and fix a mistake in the thinking phase before it propagates to the final answer.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# A problem that requires careful multi-step reasoning
problem = """
Three friends — Alice, Bob, and Carol — each have a different number of apples.
Alice has more than Bob. Carol has fewer than Bob. Together they have 18 apples.
Alice has exactly twice what Carol has. How many apples does each person have?
"""

response = client.beta.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
    max_tokens=2048,
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 8000},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": problem}],
    betas=["interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14"]
)

for block in response.content:
    if block.type == "thinking":
        print("=== THINKING (raw, exploratory) ===")
        print(block.thinking)
        print("=== END THINKING ===\n")
    elif block.type == "text":
        print("=== FINAL ANSWER (polished) ===")
        print(block.text)

# Notice: the thinking may contain trial-and-error,
# re-checking, and explicit self-correction steps.
# The final answer is clean and direct.
Chain-of-Thought vs Extended Thinking

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting asks Claude to include reasoning steps in its visible response — 'think step by step' — and the reasoning appears as part of the final text the user sees. Extended thinking is architecturally different: the reasoning phase is completely separate from the response and does not consume visible response tokens. This distinction has practical consequences. CoT advantages include compatibility with any model, full transparency to the user without extra parsing, and ease of debugging since everything is in the response text. Extended thinking advantages include the ability to reason for much longer without inflating response length, the freedom to explore dead ends without polluting the answer, and the flexibility to show or hide reasoning from the end user depending on UX requirements.
CoT: works with any model — extended thinking requires model support (currently claude-3-7-sonnet+)
CoT: reasoning and answer are in the same text block — extended thinking separates them cleanly
CoT: reasoning length increases response tokens — extended thinking uses dedicated thinking tokens
CoT: dead ends appear in the user-visible answer — extended thinking keeps dead ends hidden in thinking blocks
Extended thinking: supports longer reasoning chains without polluting the answer
Extended thinking: caller controls whether to expose thinking to end users
